original_abstract: This study provides a comprehensive overview of the role of rare earth elements (REEs) in the global economy, specifically focusing on their essential application in permanent magnets. While these technologies rely on a spectrum of elements—primarily neodymium (Nd), praseodymium (Pr), dysprosium (Dy), and cerium (Ce), alongside smaller quantities of gadolinium (Gd)—the magnet sector has emerged as the largest and fastest-growing segment of the rare earth market. Highlighting industry forecasts, such as the "Rare Earths Outlook to 2030," the research notes that magnet applications are expected to drive approximately 40% of total REE demand by the end of the decade. This surge, fueled largely by the transition to electric vehicles and renewable energy, underscores an impending supply-demand imbalance for critical magnetic materials. Finally, the paper examines the current supply chain dynamics across major global markets, including the United States, Europe, Japan, and China, while discussing the strategic necessity of advanced recycling and secondary sourcing.
humanized_abstract: Rare earth elements and permanent magnets: An analysis of current markets, future trends and the need for recycling and secondary supplies This study gives an overview of the position of the rare earth elements (REEs) in the global economy and their role in the production of permanent magnets. While permanent magnets are a mixture of a variety of elements, such as neodymium (Nd), praseodymium (Pr), dysprosium (Dy) and cerium (Ce) and smaller amounts of gadolinium (Gd), the magnet sector is by far the largest and the fastest growing part of the rare earths market. Based on studies such as Rare Earths Outlook to 2030, the magnet applications will constitute about 40% of the REEs demand by 2030. The growth of EVs and renewable energy will further increase the magnet demand, resulting in a potential supply – demand imbalance in relation to the critical magnetic materials. Finally, this paper provides an overview of the present supply situation of the main REE consuming countries, i.e. USA, Europe, Japan and China, and presents an argument for the need of advanced recycling and secondary supplies.
